content.text: When a customer reports that an AI system gave a harmful answer, silently skipped a step, or produced a hallucinated fact, the support team faces a problem that does not map neatly onto traditional bug reports. The root cause may live in a prompt, a retrieval index, a model version, or an emergent interaction between all three. A structured intake flow ensures that the right evidence is captured immediately, the issue is routed to people with the expertise to diagnose it, and customer expectations are set correctly from the first response.

A customer-reported AI issue intake flow is the end-to-end process that begins when a customer observes unexpected behaviour from an AI-powered feature and ends when that report reaches the right engineering or model-operations team with sufficient context to begin investigation. It covers the intake channel, the information collected at first contact, the classification of the issue as AI-specific or conventional, the severity assignment, and the routing decision.
Unlike a conventional software bug — where a stack trace and reproduction steps are usually enough — an AI issue requires capturing the full

: the exact prompt sent to the model, the model version and parameters, the retrieved documents or tool outputs that accompanied the request, and the verbatim model response that the customer saw. Without this context, reproduction is often impossible because the same surface-level query can produce different results under different internal conditions.

AI systems have failure modes that differ fundamentally from deterministic software. A misconfigured environment variable causes a predictable crash; a subtle prompt regression causes the model to occasionally fabricate citations or refuse valid requests. These failures are intermittent, difficult to reproduce without the original context, and often hard for customers to articulate beyond "it said something wrong."

A well-designed intake flow matters for three reasons. First, it captures the evidence before it is lost — session state, model versions, and log entries are ephemeral. Second, it routes the report to people who understand AI failure modes, not just general engineering, reducing the mean time to diagnosis. Third, it sets realistic customer expectations: AI issues often cannot be fixed with a code deploy; they may require a prompt change, a retrieval index rebuild, or a model update, all of which take different amounts of time.
Collecting the inference context at intake means engineers do not spend hours reconstructing what the model was given.
Distinguishing AI-specific issues from conventional bugs at intake prevents reports from cycling through the wrong teams.
A structured classification taxonomy accumulates a dataset of AI issue types that can reveal regressions after model or prompt updates.
A prompt, informed first response — even when the fix is weeks away — signals that the team takes AI quality seriously.
For AI systems in regulated domains, a documented intake process demonstrates due diligence when issues are reviewed by auditors or legal counsel.
A dedicated AI classification step surfaces potential safety-critical issues — harmful output, bias, PII leakage — before they are treated as low-priority general tickets.

A thorough intake flow collects more information than a standard support process, which adds friction for the customer and the support agent. If the intake form is too long, customers abandon it and report through lower-signal channels like social media. There is a tension between completeness and usability that requires deliberate calibration.
Asking customers to paste the exact prompt or session ID works only if the product UI exposes those values. Many AI products do not, making automated log capture a prerequisite.
Front-line support agents may not have the background to reliably distinguish a hallucination from a retrieval failure. Misclassification routes tickets to the wrong queue.

Even with full context, non-deterministic model behaviour means some issues cannot be reproduced on demand. A single-temperature reproduction attempt that fails does not prove the issue does not exist.
Capturing the full prompt and retrieved documents may include customer PII or confidential business data. Intake logs must be handled under the same data-handling controls as production data.
The intake flow moves through five stages:

, the customer submits the issue through a support portal, email alias, or in-app feedback widget. The system auto-stamps the submission with a timestamp, the customer account ID, and — if the product is instrumented — the session ID and feature flag state.

, the support system automatically pulls the inference log for the reported session. This log should contain the full prompt (system prompt plus user turns), the model identifier and version, sampling parameters (temperature, top-p), any retrieved documents or tool outputs, and the raw model response before any post-processing.

, the support agent or an automated classifier tags the issue as one of: hallucination, refusal, bias, data leakage, latency, capability regression, or conventional software bug. This tag drives routing.

Severity assignment applies the organisation's priority matrix: safety-critical output (harmful content, PII leakage) is immediately P1; visible quality degradation affecting many users is P2; intermittent quality issues are P3; cosmetic or single-user anomalies are P4. The
— one user, a segment, or all users — is the primary severity driver.

attrs.alt: Customer-Reported AI Issue Intake Flow diagram showing progression from customer report through intake classification, AI-specificity gate, AI triage, and severity-based routing to escalation or backlog.

content.text: The diagram shows the end-to-end intake flow. The customer report enters through any channel and passes through
where context is gathered and severity is assigned. A decision gate asks whether the issue is AI-specific. Non-AI issues go directly to the standard engineering queue. AI-specific issues enter the

stage where the inference log is retrieved and the issue is reproduced. High-severity issues (P1/P2) are escalated immediately to the AI team; lower-severity issues are queued in the AI backlog for trend analysis.
when the AI system makes consequential decisions — medical summaries, legal document analysis, financial advice — where a wrong output carries real-world harm.
for lower-stakes AI features (content recommendation, search ranking) where the same core stages apply but severity thresholds and escalation paths are less aggressive.
only if the product has not yet instrumented inference logging — but in that case, treat instrumentation as a prerequisite for production readiness.
even on products where AI is a small feature. Routing an AI hallucination to a UI bug team wastes everyone's time.
Before the intake flow can collect meaningful context automatically, the product must log every inference. At minimum, log the following fields per request:
— correlates support tickets to the exact session
— unique ID of the inference call returned by the model provider
— a hash of the system prompt so changes are detectable without logging raw PII-laden prompts
— for RAG systems, the IDs of retrieved chunks so the retrieval path can be reproduced
— temperature, top-p, max-tokens at the time of the call

import hashlib, json, time, uuid

def log_inference(session_id: str, system_prompt: str, user_message: str,
                 model_id: str, temperature: float,
                 retrieved_docs: list[str], response_text: str,
                 logger) -> str:
    """Emit a structured inference log entry and return the request_id."""
    request_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    prompt_hash = hashlib.sha256(system_prompt.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]
    entry = {
        "request_id": request_id,
        "session_id": session_id,
        "ts": time.time(),
        "model_id": model_id,
        "prompt_hash": prompt_hash,
        "temperature": temperature,
        "retrieved_doc_ids": retrieved_docs,
        "user_message_len": len(user_message),
        "response_len": len(response_text),
    }
    logger.info(json.dumps(entry))
    return request_id
Step 2: Define the AI Issue Taxonomy

# ai_issue_types.yaml
hallucination:
  description: Model states a fact that is demonstrably false
  default_severity: P3

refusal:
  description: Model refuses a valid, in-policy request
  default_severity: P3

bias_fairness:
  description: Output treats user groups differently without business justification
  default_severity: P2

pii_leakage:
  description: Output contains personal data from another user or the training set
  default_severity: P1

harmful_content:
  description: Output could cause physical, psychological, or financial harm
  default_severity: P1

capability_regression:
  description: Feature that worked previously no longer works after a model update
  default_severity: P2

latency_timeout:
  description: AI call exceeds the product SLA or times out
  default_severity: P2

from enum import Enum

class Severity(str, Enum):
    P1 = "P1"  # Safety-critical, immediate page
    P2 = "P2"  # Quality regression, 4h SLA
    P3 = "P3"  # Intermittent, 2-business-day SLA
    P4 = "P4"  # Cosmetic or single-user, backlog

BASELINE: dict[str, Severity] = {
    "pii_leakage":           Severity.P1,
    "harmful_content":       Severity.P1,
    "bias_fairness":         Severity.P2,
    "capability_regression": Severity.P2,
    "latency_timeout":       Severity.P2,
    "hallucination":         Severity.P3,
    "refusal":               Severity.P3,
}

def assign_severity(issue_type: str, affected_users: int,
                   business_impact: bool) -> Severity:
    base = BASELINE.get(issue_type, Severity.P3)
    if affected_users > 100 and base == Severity.P3:
        base = Severity.P2
    if affected_users > 1000 and base in (Severity.P2, Severity.P3):
        base = Severity.P1
    if business_impact and base == Severity.P3:
        base = Severity.P2
    return base

# P3 hallucination affecting one user
print(assign_severity("hallucination", 1, False))  # P3
# Same issue affecting 500 users with business impact
print(assign_severity("hallucination", 500, True))  # P2
P1 AI issues: immediately create a Slack alert in
and page the on-call model-ops engineer.
P2 AI issues: assign to the AI quality squad with a 4-hour first-response SLA.
P3/P4 AI issues: add to the AI backlog with a tag matching the taxonomy category; review weekly.
Non-AI issues: route to the standard engineering queue unmodified.

A SaaS legal research platform uses a RAG-based AI feature to summarise case law. A law firm customer submits a ticket: "The AI cited a 2019 Supreme Court case that does not exist. This was used in a draft brief." The intake flow handles this as follows:
The intake form auto-captures the session ID from the customer portal URL.
The support system queries the inference log and attaches the model version, temperature (0.3), and the five retrieved case-law chunk IDs to the ticket.
The classifier tags the issue as
The support agent elevates to P2 because the customer confirmed the hallucination reached an external document.

The AI quality squad reproduces the issue by re-submitting the original query and finds none of the retrieved chunks mention the fabricated case — the model generated the citation from parametric memory.
A prompt addition instructing the model to cite only case law in the provided context fixes the issue. A regression test is added to the eval suite.

Many teams instrument response logging but do not log the full prompt or retrieval context, assuming inputs are static. Prompt templates change; retrieved documents vary per user. Without the input context, diagnosis requires guesswork. Log the complete prompt — or a stable hash plus a pointer to the versioned template — alongside every response.
Treating All AI Issues as P3 by Default

A common mistake is assigning all quality issues to the lowest priority queue without a severity gate. PII leakage and harmful content discovered by a customer should never sit in a backlog for two weeks. The intake taxonomy must include an explicit P1 path for safety-critical output categories.
Not Tracking the Model Version at Intake

AI providers release new model versions continuously. If the model version is not captured at intake, there is no way to correlate a spike in hallucination reports with a provider-side model change. Always log the full model identifier including the date-stamped version string.

Customers describe what they saw, not the technical conditions that produced it. Automated context enrichment is not a convenience — it is the only reliable source of ground truth for reproducing AI issues.
Log both the raw model response and the post-processed version shown to the user. The raw response is the diagnostic artefact.
Show the session ID in the product UI.
Customers cannot supply a session ID if they cannot see it. Display it prominently so it can be copied into a support ticket.
Train support agents on AI failure modes.
A one-hour training covering hallucination, retrieval failure, prompt regression, and model capability limits allows front-line agents to classify issues correctly.
Separate AI tickets from the main engineering backlog.
Use a dedicated label or project so AI issues can be trend-analysed independently. A sudden spike in
tickets after a model update needs immediate visibility.
Feed resolved issues back into the eval suite.
Every resolved AI issue should become a regression test case, ensuring the fix holds across future model or prompt updates.
The first response should explain that AI quality fixes may require a prompt change, retrieval index update, or model provider action — each with different timelines than a code fix.
Inference log queries are the performance bottleneck in automated context enrichment. Structure logs for efficient session-based retrieval:
Partition logs by session ID prefix or date so queries scan only a small subset of data.
if storing logs in a relational or document database.
Retain high-detail inference logs for 30 days and archive compressed summaries for up to one year for audit purposes.
Run context enrichment asynchronously. The support ticket is created immediately; the enrichment job attaches the inference context within 60 seconds.
Ticketing: Jira Service Management, Linear, or Zendesk with custom fields for AI issue type and inference context link.
Inference logging: AWS CloudWatch Logs Insights, Datadog Logs, or Google Cloud Logging with structured JSON log lines.
LLM observability platforms: Langfuse, Arize AI, Weights & Biases Prompts, or Helicone — these capture inference context automatically and can surface a trace link for a given session ID.
Incident alerting: PagerDuty or OpsGenie for P1 paging; Slack webhooks for P2 channel notifications.
Eval integration: LangSmith, PromptFoo, or an internal eval harness that ingests resolved-issue queries as new test cases automatically.
Inference logs captured for support purposes contain the same data as production traffic. This creates security obligations that must be designed in from the start.
Encrypt inference logs at rest and in transit.
Apply the same encryption standards used for production customer data.
Front-line support agents should see a summary view. Full log access should require a separate role granted only to engineers actively investigating a ticket.
A 30-day rolling retention for full logs, with 12-month retention for anonymised issue type counts, balances support needs against data minimisation requirements.
Never include raw user input in Slack notifications.
P1 alerts should include severity, issue type, and a link to the ticket — not the user's query or model response, which may contain PII.
Produce an access log showing which support engineers viewed the inference context for a given ticket. This is frequently required during security reviews.
Capture the inference context — prompt hash, model version, retrieval doc IDs, sampling params — automatically at intake, before it becomes unavailable.
Classify issues as AI-specific before routing so they reach engineers who understand AI failure modes, not just general software bugs.
Apply a mandatory P1 gate for safety-critical issue types (harmful content, PII leakage) regardless of the number of affected users.
Show the session ID in the product UI so customers can supply it without requiring engineering involvement in every lookup.
Feed resolved AI issues back into the eval suite as regression tests to prevent the same issue recurring after future model or prompt updates.
Enforce strict access control on inference logs — they contain production-equivalent customer data.
Track issue counts by taxonomy category over time to detect regressions introduced by model updates, prompt changes, or retrieval index modifications.
